coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of glutathione by using the oxidase-mimicking activity of MnO2 nanosheet
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Ying
Liu, Jiaojiao
Zou, Lina
Ye, Baoxian
Li, Gaiping
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a facile ratiometric fluorescence sensor for GSH measurement was designed based on MnO2 nanosheet (NS), carbon dots (CDs), as well as a simple substrate o-phenylenediamine (OPD). Herein, MnO2 NS played triple essential roles in the sensing system. First, it could be reduced by GSH through a special reaction, and therefore served as GSH recognizer. Second, it played as a fluorescence nanoquencher to strongly quench the fluorescence of CDs. Third, it could directly oxidize OPD to yield a luminescent product 2, 3-diaminophenazine (DAP) via the intrinsic oxidase-like activity. It revealed that MnO2 NS could be reduced to Mn2+ in the presence of GSH. Thus its oxidase-like activity and fluorescence quenching abilities were inhibited, which then restricted the generation of DAP and recovered the fluorescence of CDs. Based on this phenomenon, a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor for GSH determination was fabricated by measuring the ratio of fluorescent intensity of DAP to that of CDs. Besides, the constructed ratiometric fluorescent sensor, which could be facilely operated with single-wavelength excitation, exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity with a wider linear range and a lower detection limit.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Manganese dioxide nanosheet
Carbon dots
o-Phenylenediamine
Glutathione
Single-wavelength excitation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of MnO2 NS and CDs
The principle of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing of GSH
Optimization of sensing parameters for GSH detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The fluorescence spectra of OPD (black line) and DAP (red line). Inset: photographs of OPD (left) and DAP (right) solution under 365 nm UV irradiation. (B) The fluorescence of (a) CDs, (b) CDs + MnO2 NS (5 μL) and (c) CDs + MnO2 NS (5 μL) + OPD (300 μM). The inset was the UV–visible absorption spectra of DAP (red line) and emission spectra of CDs (black line). (C) UV–visible absorption spectra of MnO2 with different concentration of GSH. (D) Feasibility of the detection of GSH with the CD@DAP-MnO2 NS sensor. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence intensity ratios (I570/I465) of CDs@DAP in the presence of different volumes of MnO2 NS. (B) Plot of fluorescence intensity at 570 nm versus different concentrations of OPD in the presence of MnO2 NS (20 μL). (C) Plot of the absorbance at 380 nm of MnO2 NS in the presence of GSH (30 μM) versus reaction time. (D) Plot of the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 570 nm in the presence of 20 μL MnO2 NS versus reaction time. The error bars are obtained via three independent experiments.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@DAP-MnO2 NS with different concentrations of GSH. (B) Plot of the I570/I465 ratio versus the concentration of GSH.

Selectivity of this ratiometric sensor for GSH detection against various interfering substances. The concentrations of Arg, Asp, Glu, Leu, Tyr, Val, MnO2 and KCl are all 500 μM, and that of GSH is 50 μM; the concentrations of AA and Cys are 5 μM.
Schematic illustration of the ratiometric sensing GSH by CDs@DAP-MnO2 NS.
Comparison with the performance of the CDs@DAP-MnO2 NS sensor with the referenced methods for GSH detection.
Recovery test results of GSH in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of glutathione by using the oxidase-mimicking activity of MnO2 nanosheet

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a facile ratiometric fluorescence sensor for GSH measurement was designed based on MnO2 nanosheet (NS), carbon dots (CDs), as well as a simple substrate o-phenylenediamine (OPD). Herein, MnO2 NS played triple essential roles in the sensing system. First, it could be reduced by GSH through a special reaction, and therefore served as GSH recognizer. Second, it played as a fluorescence nanoquencher to strongly quench the fluorescence of CDs. Third, it could directly oxidize OPD to yield a luminescent product 2, 3-diaminophenazine (DAP) via the intrinsic oxidase-like activity. It revealed that MnO2 NS could be reduced to Mn2+ in the presence of GSH. Thus its oxidase-like activity and fluorescence quenching abilities were inhibited, which then restricted the generation of DAP and recovered the fluorescence of CDs. Based on this phenomenon, a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor for GSH determination was fabricated by measuring the ratio of fluorescent intensity of DAP to that of CDs. Besides, the constructed ratiometric fluorescent sensor, which could be facilely operated with single-wavelength excitation, exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity with a wider linear range and a lower detection limit.

ce:section.ce:para: As the most prevalent intracellular biothiol among living organisms [1,2], glutathione (GSH) plays vital roles in multiple biological processes [3,4], peculiarly for regulating the redox balance and fighting free radicals [5,6]. The anomalous concentration of GSH is directly associated to a variety of diseases [7]. Therefore, exploiting facile and effective method for GSH assay is a significant subject in the analytical field.

Among previously reported approaches, fluorescence analysis has drawn significant attention owing to its wonderful advantages of simplicity, rapid response, superior sensitivity, real-time analysis [8,9]. However, most reported fluorescent systems for GSH measurement were based on the changes of single fluorescence intensity, which is susceptible to interfering factors from both environment and instrument [10]. To overcome the limitation, ratiometric fluorescence, which could largely suppress the fluctuations by using the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths as the output signal, was proposed [11,12]. As far, some commercial organic fluorescent dyes (such as scopoletin and amplex red) and quantum dots coupled with organic luminescent molecules have been used to construct the ratiometric fluorescence sensor for GSH assay [13,14]. However, these ratiometric systems required two excitation wavelengths, which rendered the detection procedures more time-consuming and complicated. Therefore, it is still significant and desirable to explore a facile ratiometric fluorescence sensor, which can be excited with a single wavelength, and possesses high sensitivity, accuracy, and selectivity for GSH detection.

Currently, a novel two-dimensional (2D) MnO2 nanosheet (NS) has attracted increasing attention owing to its superior properties, such as facile synthesis, high stability, good water solubility, low toxicity, excellent oxidase-like property [15,16]. The strong quenching abilities of MnO2 NS toward luminescent materials, as well as its special reaction with GSH, have been exploited for designing the signal-on strategy for GSH detection [17,18]. Being stimulated by such work a single wavelength excitable ratiometric fluorescence system was constructed by using the oxidase-like catalytic activity of MnO2 NS herein. On one hand, MnO2 NS could oxidize OPD to produce DAP with yellow fluorescence centered at 570 nm. On the other hand, MnO2 NS, as well as the yielded DAP, could strongly quench the emission of CDs at 465 nm. Upon mixing with GSH, MnO2 NS was reduced to Mn2+, which led to the decrease of the luminescence of DAP, accompanying with the recovery of the fluorescence of CDs. The ratio of the fluorescence intensity values of CDs and DAP was dependent on the concentration of GSH, and thus a ratiometric fluorescence sensor was fabricated.

Reduced glutathione (GSH), ascorbic acid (AA), 2-(N-Morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were obtained from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Potassium permanganate (KMnO4), citric acid, urea, potassium chloride (KCl), glutamic acid (Glu), cysteine (Cys), aspartic acid (Asp), arginine (Arg), leucine (Leu), threonine (Tyr), valine (Val) and N-2-hydroxyethylpiperazine-N′-ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the reagents were used directly without any purification. Ultrapure water with a conductivity of 18.2 MΩ cm−1 (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) and HEPES buffer (20 mM, pH 7.4) was used through this study.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to characterize the morphologies of the MnO2 NS and CDs (Tecnai G2 F20, America). AXIS Ultra DLD electron spectrometer (Kratos) recorded the powder X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) patterns. The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were performed by Lambda 35 UV–vis spectrometer (Pgeneral Instrument, Beijing, China) and 970CRT fluorescence spectrometer (Shanghai, China), respectively.

MnO2 NS was prepared according to the previous report [19]. Basically, KMnO4 (10 mmol) was added into 0.01 M MES buffer (10 mL). After being ultrasonicated for 30 min, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to collect the MnO2 NS. The precipitate was washed with ultrapure water five times, and then dried under vacuum at 50 °C for 24 h. The resulting MnO2 NS was dispersed in HEPES buffer (20 mM, pH 7.4) to produce the stock solution with concentration of 400 μg mL−1.

The hydrothermal method [20] was used to synthesize CDs. Firstly, citric acid (3.0000 g) was dissolved in 10 mL urea solution (30 wt%), and the mixture was stirred for 5 min at room temperature. Then the solution was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. The cooled mixture was purified by dialysis for about 48 h (MWCO: 1000 Da) and then the light-yellow CDs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

The detection of GSH was performed as follows: Firstly, GSH with various concentrations was added to 20 μL of MnO2 NS, and incubated at room temperature in dark for 40 min; Secondly, 20 μL of CDs (1.5 mg mL−1) and 30 μL of OPD (5 mM) were subsequently added into the above reaction solution; Finally, the mixture was diluted to 500 μL with HEPES buffer, and incubated for 30 min at room temperature to complete the reaction. Then, the fluorescence spectra of each sample were recorded upon 390 nm excitation.
The selective experiments were operated by adding other interfering substances, such as amino acid and small biomolecules, instead of GSH. The assay procedure was the same as presented above.

To estimate the practicality of the proposed approach for monitoring GSH, human serum was selected as the real sample. Human serum was diluted by 100-fold with HEPES buffer. Subsequently, various concentrations of GSH was spiked into the human serum solution. Finally, they were determined separately by the same approach as depicted above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of MnO2 NS and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Figure S1A, the obtained MnO2 NS displayed a typical 2D sheet-like morphology analogous to the previous study [21]. The chemical composition and oxidation states of MnO2 NS were investigated through XPS. As shown, there were three obvious peaks attributing to Mn 2p, O 1s, and C 1s in the full scan spectrum (Figure. S1B) [22]. The C 1s and a portion of O 1s might come from MES. For Mn 2p (Figure. S1C), the high-resolution spectra could be divided into two peaks (650.0 and 638.4 eV) with a spin-energy separation of 11.6 eV, which was similar to those of 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 electrons of MnO2 [23]. The spectra of O 1s (Figure. S1D) revealed three peaks centered at 532.1, 529.9 and 527.9 eV, corresponding to H–O–H, Mn–O–H and Mn–O–Mn, respectively [24].

As shown in Figure S2A, the prepared CDs were well-dispersed with spherical shape, and the particle size was ∼5–7 nm. The CDs solution exhibited primrose yellow color under daylight and emitted strong blue light upon 365 nm UV irradiation (inset of Figure S2A). Moreover, the CDs could be efficiently excited over the spectral ranging from 370 nm to 400 nm. With the red-shift of excitation wavelength, a slight bathochromic shift of fluorescent peaks as well as the changes in intensity were observed (Figure S2B). Upon the irradiation with 390 nm light, they revealed the maximum luminescence located at 465 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The principle of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing of GSH
ce:section.ce:para: MnO2 NS has been widely used in colorimetric systems by taking advantage of the oxidase-mimicking activity, which can directly oxidize colorimetric substrates to yield the colored product for visual detection. Herein, a typical chromogenic substrate OPD was chosen as the substrate due to that its oxidized product DAP was luminescent [25]. As illustrated in Fig. 1 A, OPD alone displayed negligible fluorescence. After incubation with MnO2 NS, its oxidation product DAP emitted strong yellow light with a peak at 570 nm upon excitation. What’s more, the colorless OPD transformed into light yellow DAP with an obvious absorption centered at 450 nm (Figure S3), which indicated that MnO2 NS could effectively catalyze the oxidization of OPD to yield DAP. More importantly, DAP could be excited efficiently upon 390 nm light, which is the maximum excitation of CDs. Meanwhile, the emission bands of DAP and CDs were obviously distinguishable, which rendered the potential possibility for ratio-metric assay with easy operation.

Taking into its easy preparation, excellent hydrophilicity, desirable biocompatibility and superior photostability, CDs, have been extensively used in sensing fields. As exhibited in Fig. 1B, the luminescence of CDs could be strongly quenched upon addition of MnO2 NS within a few seconds. Besides, the fluorescence of CDs could also be quenched by DAP through the inner filter effect owing to the partial-overlap between CDs’ emission spectra and the absorption spectrum of DAP (inset of Fig. 1B). A similar phenomenon has been also reported by Lu’s group [26].

It has been reported that the brown MnO2 NS can be directly etched to colorless Mn2+ by GSH. The special reaction can be expressed as the following formula:
2GSH + MnO2 + 2H+ = GSSG + Mn2+ + 2H2O [27]

As seen in Fig. 1C, the characteristic absorption of MnO2 NS, a broad absorption spectrum from 260 to 600 nm, decreased gradually with adding GSH, indicating the successful decomposition of MnO2 NS in the presence of GSH.

The fluorometric sensing of GSH was reasonably designed based on the triple functions of MnO2 NS: quenching the fluorescence of CDs, oxidizing OPD to yield luminescent DAP, and being decomposed by GSH selectively. As presented in Scheme 1 , MnO2 NS can directly quench the luminescence of CDs, and oxidize OPD by taking advantage of the oxidase-mimicking activity simultaneously. The produced DAP also played a positive role in quenching the fluorescence of CDs. Upon premixed with GSH, MnO2 NS can be reduced to Mn2+, and thus less DAP would be yielded. As expected, the mixture of CDs and OPD only displayed the strong luminescence of CDs at 465 nm (Fig. 1D). Upon addition of MnO2 NS, two well-resolved emission bands at 465 nm for the CDs and 570 nm for the DAP component were observed under 390 nm irradiation. After adding GSH, the fluorescence of CDs improved obviously accompanying with the decrease of the luminescence of DAP, indicating the feasibility of the designed sensing platform for GSH detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of sensing parameters for GSH detection
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve better responses, some factors, such as the initial concentration of MnO2 NS and OPD, and the reaction time, were optimized. Firstly, we studied the effect of the concentration of MnO2 NS, because MnO2 NS will quench both the fluorescence of CDs and DAP at high concentration. After adding MnO2 NS to the mixture of OPD and CDs, the luminescence of CDs at 465 nm was quenched, and a new fluorescence peak at 570 nm originating from DAP emerged. The fluorescence intensity ratio I570/I465 was found to enhance gradually with the addition of MnO2 NS in the presence of 300 μM OPD. When the added volume of MnO2 NS was 20 μL, the ratio reached a maximum (Fig. 2 A). Thus, 20 μL was selected as the optimal volume of MnO2 NS for sensing.

Secondly, OPD was oxidized by MnO2 NS to form DAP, the fluorescence of which served as the fluorometric reporter. The effect of the initial concentration of OPD on the fluorescence intensity at 570 nm was investigated in Fig. 2B. Upon addition of 20 μL MnO2 NS into the OPD liquid, the fluorescence intensity at 570 nm increased sharply when the OPD concentration increased from 100 to 200 μM, and then reached to a platform. Considering the results above, 300 μM was chosen as the working concentration of OPD in this work.

Thirdly, the reaction time for GSH and MnO2 NS was studied by monitoring the time-dependent absorption of MnO2 NS in the presence of GSH. As displayed in Fig. 2C, the absorbance of the reaction mixture decreased gradually with increasing the incubation time and reached a balance in about 40 min at room temperature. Therefore, GSH with different concentrations was premixed with MnO2 NS for 40 min to complete the special reaction, and then the reaction mixture was added to the sensing system. Due to the fact that the fluorescence of CDs can be quenched by MnO2 NS quickly, the effect of working time was estimated by measuring the fluorescence of DAP at 570 nm. Fig. 2D exhibited that the fluorescence response reached stable in 30 min in presence of MnO2 NS. Therefore, 30 min was set as the incubation time for the subsequent fluorescence ratiometric determination of GSH.

To confirm the applicability of the designed approach for fluorescence ratiometric sensing of GSH, the responses of the sensing platform to GSH with various concentrations were investigated under the optimal conditions. As displayed in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence of CDs enhanced with increasing GSH concentration, while that of DAP possessed inverse responses, which were consistent with our expectation. Furthermore, the value of fluorescence intensity ratio I570/I465 had a good linear relationship with the concentration of GSH ranging from 1 to 100 μM (Fig. 3B). The equation of linear regression was I570/I465 = 1.8739–0.0104C/μM (R2 = 0.9915), and the calculated limit of detection (LOD) for this assay is 270 nM (3σ). The sensitivity of the proposed approach is better than or similar with the majority of previously optical sensors as summarized in Table 1 . Although the LOD is higher than the two fluorescence ratiometric ones, the linear range is obviously wider in our work. It is a desirable property in terms of measuring the level of GSH due to its naturally high concentration gradient in different biological samples. For example, the level of intracellular GSH is over 3 orders of magnitude higher than that of extracellular (e.g. 2 μM in red plasma [28], 2 mM in erythrocytes [29], 10 mM in liver cells [30]).

For assessing whether the proposed platform is selective for detecting GSH, some potential influence, including KCl, amino acids (Arg, Asp, Glu, Leu, Tyr, Val and Cys), and ascorbic acid (AA) were studied in aqueous solution. Due to the level of GSH is generally much higher than Cys and AA in human serum, the concentration of AA and Cys was chosen only 1/10 of GSH. As presented in Fig. 4 , these substances have almost no influence on the detection of GSH and the interference of AA and Cys on this system can be ignored, demonstrating an excellent selectivity of this fluorescence ratiometric platform for GSH sensing.

Due to the satisfied sensitivity and selectivity, we further tested its capability to sense GSH in serum samples by the standard addition method. As shown in Table 2 , the quantitative recoveries for GSH in serum samples were in the range from 97.3% to 101.1%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of five samples was lower than 3.1%. These results implied that the proposed fluorescent platform can be used as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor with high analytical performance for GSH detection in real samples.

In conclusion, a facile ratiometric fluorescent sensor for GSH was constructed on the basis of the oxidase-like activity and fluorescence quenching ability of MnO2 NS, and the special reduction decomposition of the MnO2 NS by GSH. The two fluorescent reporters (CDs and DAP) could be effectively excited at a single wavelength, and revealed two distinguishable luminescent peaks. This made it possible to easily operate the ratiometric fluorescence assay and achieve more accurate results over the single emission sensors. The sensor constituents CDs and MnO2 NS were easily to synthesize, making the sensor simplicity, low cost, and convenient operation. Furthermore, the obtained wider linear range and the lower LOD were desirable for measuring the level of GSH in different biological samples.

Ying Cao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Jiaojiao Liu: Validation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation. Lina Zou: Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation. Baoxian Ye: Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation. Gaiping Li: Data curation, Writing - review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21575130).
